heading: TONUS SECUNDUS
card_summary: This chunk opens the Tonus Secundus with its first chapter, "Quanta dissonantia absoni reperiantur mundi sapientes in suis dogmatibus" (How great a dissonance is found among the world's wise men in their doctrines). Zorzi catalogues the irreconcilable disagreements among pagan philosophical schools — Socratic, Aristotelian, Epicurean, Pythagorean, and Islamic — as evidence that secular wisdom fractures rather than harmonizes. Specific examples cited include Aristippus vs. Ariston on the summum bonum, Alexander of Aphrodisias vs. Averroes on the soul and intellect, Aristotle's rupture with Plato, the Saracen Algazel (al-Ghazali) against all philosophers, and Averroes against Algazel. The argument sets up the Tonus Secundus by contrasting the dissonance of worldly sages with the harmony achievable only through revealed wisdom.
essay:
## Tonus Secundus, Caput I: The Dissonance of the World's Philosophers

### The Musical Metaphor as Structural Argument

The *Tonus Secundus* opens with an extended application of Zorzi's governing musical metaphor to the domain of pagan and secular philosophy. The chapter title — "Quanta dissonantia absoni reperiantur mundi sapientes in suis dogmatibus" — announces the inversion of the harmony theme that organizes *De Harmonia Mundi* as a whole. Here, harmony is defined by its absence: the "mundi sapientes," the wise men of this world, produce not concord but catastrophic dissonance.

Zorzi opens with a definition of harmony drawn explicitly from musical theory:

> *harmonia, quae (ut musici diffiniunt) est concentus nervorum, aut vocum in integros modos sine aliqua offensione consonantium*

<cite>p. 332</cite>

This definition — harmony as the consonance of strings or voices in complete modes without any offense — is offered as the standard the pagan philosophers manifestly fail to meet. The phrase "sine aliqua offensione" is significant: it implies that dissonance is not merely aesthetic failure but a kind of moral and cognitive offense, a wounding of right order.

### The Catalogue of Philosophical Discord

The bulk of the chapter is a pointed doxographical catalogue of irreconcilable disagreements within and between the major philosophical schools. Zorzi's method here is polemical rather than neutral: each example is chosen to show that secular reason, even at its most refined, reproduces conflict rather than truth.

**Socratic disagreements:** Aristippus and Ariston, both pupils of Socrates, are shown to have placed the human *finis* — the ultimate end of human life — in diametrically opposed locations: Aristippus in pleasure (*voluptas*), Ariston in virtue (*virtus*). This is not peripheral disagreement but a fracture at the root of ethics.

**Aristotelian fractures:** Zorzi notes that Aristotle's own leading followers, Alexander of Aphrodisias and Averroes, "split his doctrine and make a fork" ("eius dogma scindentes bivium faciunt"), above all on the question of the soul (*in negotio animae*): Alexander asserting the soul's mortality, Averroes asserting a single unified intellect common to all human beings. Both, Zorzi concludes, "dissent not only from each other but from the truth" ("qui non modo inter se, sed a veritate dissident"). The double dissent — from each other and from reality — is structurally important: the disagreement is not merely contingent but symptomatic of philosophy's constitutive inability to reach the truth unaided.

**Aristotle against Plato:** Zorzi then moves to the most dramatically charged case, Aristotle's rupture with his own teacher:

> *Aristoteles autem auditor Platonis uno anno supra viginti a doctrina, et moribus praeceptoris adeo dissentit, ut eum criminetur, et falsis calumniis prosequatur.*

<cite>p. 332</cite>

Twenty-one years a student of Plato, Aristotle not only departs from his teacher's doctrine and conduct but, in Zorzi's telling, actively slanders him with false accusations. This is the severest charge in the catalogue — philosophical disagreement shading into moral failure.

**Islamic philosophy:** Zorzi extends his survey to the Islamic philosophical tradition with two figures. "Saracenus Algazel" (al-Ghazali, 1058–1111) is said to "rise up against all philosophers" — a reference to the *Tahafut al-Falasifa* (The Incoherence of the Philosophers). Averroes in turn attacks Algazel, but Zorzi dismisses this counter-attack with a vivid simile:

> *sed velut coluber in ipso conatu frangitur*

<cite>p. 332–333</cite>

"But like a snake, he breaks himself in the very attempt." The image is striking: Averroes is not refuted so much as self-defeated, broken by his own contortion. The inclusion of al-Ghazali and Averroes signals Zorzi's genuinely encyclopedic scope — the dissonance of worldly wisdom is not a specifically Greek or Latin phenomenon but universal.

**Epicurean self-contradiction:** Lucretius and the Epicureans are shown to disagree even with themselves: in one place affirming that the gods have commerce with mortals, in another denying it. This internal inconsistency is a stronger indictment even than inter-school conflict.

**Pythagorean schism:** The Pythagoreans, whose harmonic cosmology is in many respects a precursor to Zorzi's own project, are said to be "cut into several utterly discordant parts" on the question of the transmigration of souls (*animarum transmigrationem*). The irony is pointed: the school most associated with musical and mathematical harmony is internally riven.

### Rhetorical and Theological Function

The chapter functions as a proem for the entire *Tonus Secundus*, establishing the negative foil against which Zorzi will develop his positive account of divinely grounded harmony. The phrase "mundi sapientes" echoes the Pauline critique of worldly wisdom (1 Corinthians 1:20–25), and Zorzi's enumeration of philosophical disagreements works as an implicit argument for the necessity of revealed knowledge — Scripture, Kabbalah, and the Christian tradition — as the only framework capable of producing genuine concord. The musical metaphor is thus not merely decorative: the very definition of harmony ("concentus...sine aliqua offensione consonantium") becomes an implicit criterion that pagan philosophy, for all its sophistication, cannot satisfy.

### Connection to Zorzi's Larger Project

The *Tonus Secundus* takes up the question of first principles ("TRA I PRINCÌPI DELLE COSE"), and this opening chapter clears the ground by demonstrating that secular philosophical traditions — Platonic, Aristotelian, Epicurean, Pythagorean, and Islamic — have all failed to achieve consensus on fundamental questions: the summum bonum, the nature of the soul, the existence and nature of the divine. This failure prepares the reader for Zorzi's synthetic project: a harmonization drawn from Hebrew Scripture, Kabbalistic tradition, Neoplatonism, and Christian theology that, unlike the dissonant pagan schools, can produce genuine intellectual and spiritual concord.
